requirements_for_qualification.summary: Must make non-deductible traditional IRA contribution and convert to Roth IRA to circumvent Roth IRA income limits, enabling high-income earners to build tax-free retirement savings through backdoor strategy
criteria: Income exceeds Roth IRA contribution phase-out limits ($146,000-$161,000 single; $230,000-$240,000 MFJ for 2024)
Have earned income enabling traditional IRA contribution ($7,000 for 2024; $8,000 if age 50+)
Make non-deductible traditional IRA contribution (Form 8606 required to track basis)
Convert traditional IRA to Roth IRA shortly after contribution to minimize taxable gain
Consider pro-rata rule if existing pre-tax traditional, SEP, or SIMPLE IRA balances exist
Must have no or minimal existing traditional IRA balances to avoid pro-rata taxation on conversion
Can be done annually as long-term strategy for tax-free retirement savings accumulation
Coordinate with spouse to double annual Roth accumulation ($14,000 or $16,000 if both age 50+)

backdoor_roth_rules.income_limit_workaround: Allows Roth contributions despite income exceeding direct contribution limits
backdoor_roth_rules.contribution_limit: $7,000 per person ($8,000 if age 50+) can be backdoored annually
backdoor_roth_rules.minimal_tax_if_clean: If no existing IRA balances and immediate conversion, minimal tax on conversion
backdoor_roth_rules.tax_free_growth: Once in Roth IRA, grows tax-free and distributions tax-free in retirement
backdoor_roth_rules.no_rmd_requirement: Roth IRAs not subject to required minimum distributions during owner's lifetime
backdoor_roth_rules.spousal_strategy: Each spouse can execute backdoor Roth for household total of $14,000-$16,000 annually

restrictions: Pro-rata rule applies if existing traditional, SEP, or SIMPLE IRA balances; can't cherry-pick non-deductible portion
Pro-rata calculation uses 12/31 year-end balances of all traditional IRAs to determine taxable portion
Step transaction doctrine risk if contribution and conversion too close together; wait prudent period (30+ days)
Must file Form 8606 annually to track non-deductible contributions and basis for future distributions
Cannot deduct traditional IRA contribution if income exceeds deductibility limits and covered by employer plan
Conversion creates taxable income if pro-rata rule applies; may push into higher brackets
State tax treatment varies; some states tax Roth conversions even when federal tax minimal
5-year rule applies to converted amounts; early withdrawal of converted principal may trigger penalty
